metadata_frame: The following passage comes from The Lowell Lectures on the Ascent of man, a science and religion lectures published in 1894 by Henry Drummond, a Scottish evangelist, scientist, writer, lecturer.
main_question:
The new system worked better, it spread, and was finally adopted by those higher nations which it also helped to create. It is an instance, nevertheless, of the slowness with which radical changes succeed in leaving great masses of mankind, that the older system, with the ban of Evolution upon it, still survives in Modern Europe. [masked sentence revising an implied expectation]

Write a sentence appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked sentence revising an implied expectation]:
source_title: The Lowell Lectures on the Ascent of man
answer_strings: Yet there are signs, even among the uncivilized, that polygamy is passing away.
Yet there are signs, even among the civilized, that polygamy is coming into being.

But to fix the eye upon it at that remote stage and assert that, because it was apparently then automatic, it must therefore have been automatic ever after, is to forget the progressive character of Evolution as well as to ignore facts.
But it is not so much in ignoring Man that evolutionary philosophy has gone astray; for of that error it has seriously begun to repent.
One would expect rapid progress but change moves glacially through society.
But the old system persists, stubbornly resisting the new order's sweeping promise.
passage: The new system worked better, it spread, and was finally adopted by those higher nations which it also helped to create. It is an instance, nevertheless, of the slowness with which radical changes succeed in leaving great masses of mankind, that the older system, with the ban of Evolution upon it, still survives in Modern Europe. Yet there are signs, even among the uncivilized, that polygamy is passing away.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from The Lowell Lectures on the Ascent of man, a science and religion discourse published in 1894 by Henry Drummond, a Scottish evangelist, scientist, writer, and lecturer.
main_question:
In "fixing on a part" whereby to "reconstruct the ultimate," it has fixed upon a part which is not the most vital part, and the reconstructions, therefore, have come to be wholly out of focus. Fix upon the wrong "part," and the instability of the fabric built upon it is a foregone conclusion. Now, [masked clause acknowledging a countervailing fact]. And although it anticipates much that we should prefer to leave untouched until it appears in its natural setting, the gravity of the issues makes it essential to summarize the whole situation now.

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause acknowledging a countervailing fact]:
source_title: The Lowell Lectures on the Ascent of man
answer_strings: although reconstructions of the cosmos in the light of Evolution are the chief feature of the science of our time, in almost no case does even a hint of the true scientific stand-point appear to be perceived

although reconstructions of the cosmos in the light of Evolution are not the chief feature of the science of our time, in almost every case does even a hint of the true scientific standpoint appear to be perceived
though its bearing upon this or that individual case may seem unjust, its necessity for the world as a whole is vindicated

though the initial focus may seem misdirected, the very act of dissecting these fragments provides insight that, paradoxically, fortifies our understanding of the broader tapestry, revealing hidden interconnections that would otherwise remain obscured within the narrative
passage: In "fixing on a part" whereby to "reconstruct the ultimate," it has fixed upon a part which is not the most vital part, and the reconstructions, therefore, have come to be wholly out of focus. Fix upon the wrong "part," and the instability of the fabric built upon it is a foregone conclusion. Now, although reconstructions of the cosmos in the light of Evolution are the chief feature of the science of our time, in almost no case does even a hint of the true scientific stand-point appear to be perceived. And although it anticipates much that we should prefer to leave untouched until it appears in its natural setting, the gravity of the issues makes it essential to summarize the whole situation now.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from The Lowell Lectures on the Ascent of man, a science and religion discourse published in 1894 by Henry Drummond, a Scottish evangelist, scientist, writer, and lecturer.
main_question:
Dazzled with the apparent solidity of this foundation, men made haste to run up a structure which included the whole vast range of life-vegetal, animal, social-based on a law which explained but half the facts, and was only relevant, in the crude form in which it was universally stated, for the childhood of the world. It was impossible for such an edifice to stand. Natural history cannot in any case cover the whole facts of human history, and, so interpreted, can only fatally distort them. The mistake had been largely qualified had Mr. Darwin's followers even accepted his foundation in its first integrity; [masked clause revising an implied expectation]. Mr. Darwin's sagacity led him distinctly to foresee that narrow interpretations of his great phrase "Struggle for Existence" were certain to be made; and in the opening chapters of the Origin of Species, he warns us that the term must be applied in its "large and metaphorical sense, including dependence of one being on another, and including (which is more important) not only the life of the individual, but success in leaving progeny."

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause revising an implied expectation]:
source_title: The Lowell Lectures on the Ascent of man
answer_strings: but, perhaps because the author of the theory himself but dimly apprehended the complement of his thesis, few seem to have perceived that anything was amiss
but, perhaps because the author of the theory himself clearly grasped the implications of his thesis, many seem to have perceived that something was amiss
but he cannot escape a purpose-the purpose, conscious or unconscious, of unfolding the purpose which lies behind the facts which he narrates
however, even those who initially embraced Darwin's doctrine in its form would have soon realized that the underlying flaw could not be reconciled with empirical reality
passage: Dazzled with the apparent solidity of this foundation, men made haste to run up a structure which included the whole vast range of life-vegetal, animal, social-based on a law which explained but half the facts, and was only relevant, in the crude form in which it was universally stated, for the childhood of the world. It was impossible for such an edifice to stand. Natural history cannot in any case cover the whole facts of human history, and, so interpreted, can only fatally distort them. The mistake had been largely qualified had Mr. Darwin's followers even accepted his foundation in its first integrity; but, perhaps because the author of the theory himself but dimly apprehended the complement of his thesis, few seem to have perceived that anything was amiss. Mr. Darwin's sagacity led him distinctly to foresee that narrow interpretations of his great phrase "Struggle for Existence" were certain to be made; and in the opening chapters of the Origin of Species, he warns us that the term must be applied in its "large and metaphorical sense, including dependence of one being on another, and including (which is more important) not only the life of the individual, but success in leaving progeny."

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from The Lowell Lectures on the Ascent of man, a science and religion discourse published in 1894 by Henry Drummond, a Scottish evangelist, scientist, writer, and lecturer.
main_question:
For these dates show that each animal in each particular country chooses that time of the year to give birth to her young when they will have the best chance of surviving-that is to say, when the climate is mildest, food most abundant, and the prospects of life on the whole most favorable. [masked sentence describing an inference or effect] Because those born at this season survived and those born out of it perished, by the prolonged action of Natural Selection these dates in time probably became engrained in the species, and would only alter with climate itself.

Write a sentence appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked sentence describing an inference or effect]:
source_title: The Lowell Lectures on the Ascent of man
answer_strings: The dormouse thus brings forth its young in August, when the nuts begin to ripen; and the young deer sees the light just before the first grass shoots into greenness.
Nature must deepen the result by extending it to the time before marriage.
It follows that animals have evolved to give birth during specific times of year as a consequence of natural selection favoring survival and reproduction under optimal conditions.

Hence, across species in the reproductive calendar was fixed by natural selection, aligning birth with the mildest season to maximize offspring survival and to ensure the continuation of favorable traits.
passage: For these dates show that each animal in each particular country chooses that time of the year to give birth to her young when they will have the best chance of surviving-that is to say, when the climate is mildest, food most abundant, and the prospects of life on the whole most favorable. The dormouse thus brings forth its young in August, when the nuts begin to ripen; and the young deer sees the light just before the first grass shoots into greenness. Because those born at this season survived and those born out of it perished, by the prolonged action of Natural Selection these dates in time probably became engrained in the species, and would only alter with climate itself.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from The Lowell Lectures on the Ascent of man, a science and religion discourse published in 1894 by Henry Drummond, a Scottish evangelist, scientist, writer, and lecturer.
main_question:
Man owes what Burns calls his "heaven-erected face" to the Struggle for Life. How recent this change is, how new the attitude still is to him, is seen from the simple fact that even yet he has not attained the power of retaining the erect position long. Most men sit down when they can, and so unnatural is the standing position, [masked clause describing an inference or effect]. Possibly both the erect position and the Club had another origin, but the detail is immaterial.

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause describing an inference or effect]:
source_title: The Lowell Lectures on the Ascent of man
answer_strings: so unstable the equilibrium, that when slightly sick or faint, Man cannot stand at all
so stable the equilibrium, that when slightly sick or faint, Man can stand firm

so that should it happen that a combination of these precise centres had never been made before, it is obvious that no routes could possibly exist between them, and these must then and there be prospected
so they find it impossible to sustain the posture, revealing their inherent fragility and fleeting resolve
passage: Man owes what Burns calls his "heaven-erected face" to the Struggle for Life. How recent this change is, how new the attitude still is to him, is seen from the simple fact that even yet he has not attained the power of retaining the erect position long. Most men sit down when they can, and so unnatural is the standing position, so unstable the equilibrium, that when slightly sick or faint, Man cannot stand at all. Possibly both the erect position and the Club had another origin, but the detail is immaterial.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from The Lowell Lectures on the Ascent of man, a science and religion discourse published in 1894 by Henry Drummond, a Scottish evangelist, scientist, writer, and lecturer.
main_question:
The tenement in fact consists now of innumerable groups of cells congregated together, suites of apartments as it were, which have quickly arranged themselves in symmetrical, definite, and withal different forms. Were these forms not different as well as definite we should hardly call it an evolution, nor should we characterize the resulting aggregation as a higher organism. A hundred cottages placed in a row would never form a castle. What makes the castle superior to the hundred cottages is not the number of its rooms, [masked clause describing a cause or reason]. It lies in the number and nature and variety of useful purposes to which the rooms are put, the perfection with which each is adapted to its end, and the harmonious co-operation among them with reference to some common work.

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause describing a cause or reason]:
source_title: The Lowell Lectures on the Ascent of man
answer_strings: for they are possibly fewer; nor their difference in shape, for that is immaterial
for every animal whether it should increase, or change, or die
for the most part timid and defenceless as individuals, have survived to occupy in untold multitudes the highest places in Nature
because of their unique arrangement and interconnection
in order to maximize functional synergy among rooms, they are meticulously arranged for performance
passage: The tenement in fact consists now of innumerable groups of cells congregated together, suites of apartments as it were, which have quickly arranged themselves in symmetrical, definite, and withal different forms. Were these forms not different as well as definite we should hardly call it an evolution, nor should we characterize the resulting aggregation as a higher organism. A hundred cottages placed in a row would never form a castle. What makes the castle superior to the hundred cottages is not the number of its rooms, for they are possibly fewer; nor their difference in shape, for that is immaterial. It lies in the number and nature and variety of useful purposes to which the rooms are put, the perfection with which each is adapted to its end, and the harmonious co-operation among them with reference to some common work.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from The Lowell Lectures on the Ascent of man, a science and religion discourse published in 1894 by Henry Drummond, a Scottish evangelist, scientist, writer, and lecturer.
main_question:
Is the fact that no novelty is introduced in the case of Man a proof that the ultimate Hand has appeared? By no means. And yet it is probable for other reasons that the ultimate Hand has appeared; that there will never be a more perfectly handed animal than Man. And why? [masked sentence describing a cause or reason] In the perfecting of the bodily organs, as of all other mechanical devices, necessity is the mother of invention.

Write a sentence appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked sentence describing a cause or reason]:
source_title: The Lowell Lectures on the Ascent of man
answer_strings: Because the causes which up to this point have furthered the evolution of the Hand have begun to cease to act.
Because movement is work, and work is exercise, and exercise may mean a further evolution of the part of him that is exercised.
The rationale is that evolution has already produced an organism with such a high level of dexterity.
Because the evolutionary purpose of humanity is to attain perfection, the ultimate Hand has manifested through relentless natural selection ever.
passage: Is the fact that no novelty is introduced in the case of Man a proof that the ultimate Hand has appeared? By no means. And yet it is probable for other reasons that the ultimate Hand has appeared; that there will never be a more perfectly handed animal than Man. And why? Because the causes which up to this point have furthered the evolution of the Hand have begun to cease to act. In the perfecting of the bodily organs, as of all other mechanical devices, necessity is the mother of invention.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from The Lowell Lectures on the Ascent of man, a science and religion discourse published in 1894 by Henry Drummond, a Scottish evangelist, scientist, writer, and lecturer.
main_question:
Plant in the mind, or evoke from it, the idea of acting in a given way with reference to some half-dozen daily trifles when done in the presence of one authoritative individual, and Evolution has already found something to work on. The children have got six, if not ten commandments. Extend the half-dozen things done rightly to a whole. dozen, and then to a score, and then to a hundred; and let it become habitual to do these things rightly. When the right doing of these things commends the doer to one person, he will next be apt to commend himself by similar conduct to other persons, [masked clause describing a condition or proviso]. Whether good behavior purchases favor or simply succeeds in evading penalties is at first immaterial.

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause describing a condition or proviso]:
source_title: The Lowell Lectures on the Ascent of man
answer_strings: if their standard happens to be the same
if Nature wishes to make caretakers
if the course of Evolution holds on its way
unless he finds himself in the presence of another authoritative figure
passage: Plant in the mind, or evoke from it, the idea of acting in a given way with reference to some half-dozen daily trifles when done in the presence of one authoritative individual, and Evolution has already found something to work on. The children have got six, if not ten commandments. Extend the half-dozen things done rightly to a whole. dozen, and then to a score, and then to a hundred; and let it become habitual to do these things rightly. When the right doing of these things commends the doer to one person, he will next be apt to commend himself by similar conduct to other persons, if their standard happens to be the same. Whether good behavior purchases favor or simply succeeds in evading penalties is at first immaterial.
